title: Better Pauli Channel Learning with Maximum Likelihood Estimation
authors: Daniel Belkin, Faisal Alam, Matthew Thibodeau, Alireza Seif, Ewout van den Berg, Bryan K. Clark
abstract: Error mitigation in a noisy quantum device requires a very good estimate of the noise channel. The accuracy of probabilistic error cancellation is often limited by the high sample complexity of channel tomography. In principle, optimal sample complexity is attained by maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), but MLE is computationally challenging. We show that MLE can be made computationally tractable in certain cases of interest. For the common case of a 1D-local sparse Pauli-Lindblad channel, the likelihood function reduces to an efficiently-evaluable Bayesian network. We show that the resulting computation leads to substantially improved tomography. In addition, we demonstrate by simulation that this can lead to meaningful improvements to the overhead of error mitigation. We also discuss possible extensions of our algorithm to more general settings, such as non-1D circuits and non-Pauli errors.
summary: This paper addresses the difficulty of characterizing noise in quantum computers by proposing an efficient Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) technique. By mapping the complex likelihood function for 1D Pauli noise channels onto a tractable Bayesian network, the authors achieve significantly better noise parameter estimation than previous methods. This advancement promises more reliable error mitigation protocols for near-term quantum devices.
main_problem: The primary challenge is accurately estimating the unknown noise channel in noisy quantum devices, as standard tomography methods suffer from high sample complexity.
main_result: The authors show that Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) can be made computationally tractable for 1D-local sparse Pauli-Lindblad channels by mapping the likelihood function to an efficiently-evaluable Bayesian network, leading to substantially improved tomography.
method: The core method involves reformulating the likelihood function for the noise parameters into a Bayesian network structure, allowing for efficient inference using belief propagation, which outperforms empirical estimators like EPF.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum computation process corrupted by a Pauli-Lindblad noise channel, modeled specifically as a 1D-local sparse Pauli channel. The analysis compares MLE against Empirical Pauli Fidelities (EPF) for parameter estimation.
key_observables: Mean squared difference between learned and true noise parameters ($\omega_i$), and the resulting fidelity/magnetization of error-mitigated observables.
important_parameters: System size (number of qubits), circuit depth, and the noise parameters $\{w_i\}$ defining the Pauli-Lindblad channel.
main_assumptions: The noise is assumed to be Pauli-Lindblad, 1D-local, and sparse. The computational tractability relies on mapping the likelihood to a Bayesian network structure.
figures_summary: Figures compare MLE vs. EPF sample complexity (requiring MLE to use significantly fewer samples) and illustrate the mathematical reduction from quantum tensor networks to classical Bayesian network probability distributions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for MLE over EPF, then deriving the computational simplification for 1D Pauli channels using Bayesian networks, and finally demonstrating the superior sample complexity and accuracy of the MLE approach through simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, computationally feasible pathway to perform high-precision quantum process tomography, which is crucial for building reliable quantum hardware and understanding decoherence mechanisms in solid-state or superconducting qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of our work. We give an algorithm that allows MLE to be used for channel learning. This gives better learning of noise models than previous approaches. When used for PEC on a quantum circuit, the resulting noise model also gives markedly better error-mitigated outputs.

FIG. 2. Average magnetization over time in a simulation of a Trotterized transverse-field Ising model circuit on 10 qubits. The noise channel is a 1D 2-local Pauli-Lindblad channel with random coefficients sampled uniformly from (0, 10−3). PEC is done using channel estimates learned with either MLE or EPF on a training set of 999,999 samples. Shaded regions show standard error. See Section IV B for further details.

FIG. 3. Schematic illustrating a typical data collection setup for learning gate-based Pauli errors. A layer of CZ gates induces noise N. We assume noiseless single-qubit rotations Ri can be used to prepare states and rotate to a desired measurement basis.

FIG. 4. (a) Original tensor network. (b) After commuting the basis rotations through the errors. (c) Reduction rules map quantum states to classical probability distributions. (d) Error channel as evolution of a classical distribution.

FIG. 5. (a) Tensor network describing classical probability distribution preparation and measurement. (b) Bayesian network representation of the same distribution. (c) Simplified tensor network with single-site errors separated out and uninformative bits outlined in yellow. (d) Corresponding reduced Bayesian network.
